body: Backfill summary: This provenance project documents the Provenance product itself -- the research layer of Statesman. The root requirement decomposes into 8 children covering data model, Convex backend, CLI, web UI, traceability, broker sync, threads, and auth. 8 source documents are linked (architecture docs, specs, plans). 5 key design resolutions capture decisions on Convex, ELK, Focus+Context, recursive requirements, and one-directional sync. 6 rules codify the enforceable outputs. The implementation spans 56 git commits, ~5,500 lines of documentation, and production deployment on Railway + Convex Cloud.

The data model evolved from the Dreadnought spec (session-based, 4 types: Source/Session/Decision/Rule) into Provenance's recursive model (Source/Requirement/Resolution/Rule). Key evolution: sessions became recursive requirements, decisions became resolutions as independent graph nodes. The Convex schema has 10 tables with 27 indexes. Topics, questions, and boundaries live in separate tables (not embedded) for granular reactivity and to avoid the 1MB Convex document limit. 9 typed graph edge types model relationships between artifacts.

The UI was built in 8 phases: basic React Flow canvas, ELK auto-layout with zone partitioning, internal nodes (topics/questions/boundaries), expandable cards with thread integration, progressive node detail on zoom, semantic zoom drill-down via View Transitions API, Focus+Context depth view (selected after evaluating 8 prototype approaches), and creation tools with empty states. 24 component files in src/components/provenance/. Key technical choices: Source Serif 4 typography, flat URL structure, View Transitions API for drill animations.

Backend implemented in 6 phases: (1) Foundation -- Convex project, schema, auth, ConvexProvider; (2) Sources and Requirements CRUD with graph edges; (3) Topics, Questions, and Refinement workflows; (4) Resolutions and Rules with status lifecycles; (5) Threads and Conversations attachable to any artifact; (6) Broker Sync with traceability queries, HTTP endpoints, and hourly cron job. graph.ts (1,461 lines) provides 9 query functions including getCanvasScope, getDepthScope, getRootOverview, traceForward/Backward, findGaps/Stale/Orphans, and getCoverageStats.

The provenance traceability value shouldn't be gated on adopting the Statesman contract framework. Three cases drive this: (1) existing test suites that work fine and won't be rewritten, (2) tests outside Statesman's scope like perf tests or specific unit tests, (3) teams that want traceability but can't afford the time investment of rewriting tests. The idea is source-code annotations that declare which rule a test enforces, with a scanner that extracts the links.

Agreed on static analysis first. The annotation format is the foundation -- comment-based (// @statesman-rule RULE-CODE) works in any language with zero dependencies. Native decorators/macros are ergonomic sugar on top. The scanner is a single tool to build vs N framework integrations for runtime reporters. Key design constraint: the annotation format and Provenance coverage API must accept runtime evidence later without breaking the static-only path. Decomposed into 6 sub-requirements: annotation format, scanner, CLI integration, coverage model extension, stale detection, and runtime extension point.

Allure's label/link primitive model is the right architecture but we should use Statesman's own metadata vocabulary, not Allure's PM concepts. Contracts already carry name/description/tags on the trait and coverage_level/confidence/notes on the RuleContract join. External annotations should mirror those exact fields so the coverage model is consistent regardless of source. No epic/feature/story -- Statesman is strategically not a project management tool.

Building the scanner into the Statesman CLI means external tests get the full Provenance ecosystem for free: traceability queries, impact analysis (traceForward shows affected tests when sources change), gap detection (rules with no coverage from any source), Provenance UI visibility alongside contracts, audit trails on all coverage reports, and CI integration as a single pipeline step. The pitch is not just traceability -- it is that your entire existing test suite becomes visible in the same graph as everything else.

Coverage should be first-class under 'statesman coverage', not buried under provenance. It spans both worlds. When definitive changes go into source code, post diffs to rule threads -- new coverage added, coverage removed, confidence changed. Dry-runs should not post.

Folded into the scanner requirement (jn73b66tg0w75ansvea8bmzgz182mg4a). Stale detection is a validation step during scan, not a separate capability. The scanner cross-references annotations against active rules and warns on mismatches.

Deliberately left in discovery. The key constraint for now: the broker API for coverage (RuleContract) and the annotation format must not require runtime fields. Pass/fail, timestamp, duration should be optional extensions that runtime reporters can provide later without breaking the static-only path. No active planning needed until the scanner ships and we see adoption patterns.

test ping

Research complete: Provenance MCP Agent Integration (docs/research/2026-03-29-provenance-mcp-agent-integration.md)

Key findings:
- MCP (Model Context Protocol) has been adopted by every major AI platform (Claude, ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Amazon Q, 100+ clients). One MCP server reaches all of them.
- The recommended architecture requires zero new infrastructure: a single /mcp HTTP route added to the existing Provenance Convex deployment as a stateless JSON-only protocol adapter. It dispatches MCP tool calls to existing Convex functions directly.
- Authentication uses the same Bearer JWT the existing /api/* routes validate. OAuth Authorization Code + PKCE endpoints would be added to the control plane — same WorkOS flow it already does for web and CLI.
- The in-progress agent auth RBAC plan (Phase 1-4) provides fine-grained permission scoping that maps directly to MCP tool visibility: read-only users see query tools, contributors see create/discuss tools, analysts get everything except approve.
- SSE streaming is NOT required for Claude.ai or ChatGPT — JSON-only mode is a first-class MCP pattern and sufficient for all Provenance operations.
- Claude mobile access works via claude.ai in mobile browser with Custom Connectors.
- Convex HTTP actions support the MCP SDK's WebStandardStreamableHTTPServerTransport (standard Request/Response interface).

Open questions: OAuth endpoint specifics on control plane, MCP SDK compatibility with Convex runtime, tool count/granularity decisions.

Follow-up: Retraced research using Provenance-first approach.

Key finding: the knowledge graph would have materially improved the MCP integration research if consulted first. Specifically:
1. The 8 RBAC resolutions (WorkOS RBAC directly, broker stays simple, agent token inheritance, delegation support) would have answered auth architecture questions without reading 1000 lines of implementation plan.
2. The source refs on the second RBAC requirement (jn7626ddcmx4geff19bhd89q1x83t13q) link to agent-auth-rbac-provenance.md, which references the session trails brainstorming doc -- the doc I missed and had to be pointed to manually.
3. The backfill message on this thread provides project orientation that took multiple sub-agent searches to reconstruct from source files.

Corrected research approach for future: (1) query Provenance graph first for requirements, sources, resolutions, threads (2) then go to codebase for implementation verification (3) then web research for external context (4) write back to Provenance. The graph is the index; the codebase is the implementation.

New brainstorming doc: Provenance Graph Navigability (docs/brainstorming/2026-03-29-provenance-graph-navigability.md, source k17fpgmt9q34fj1t9bm6ya32bh83tbbk).

Captures four related navigability problems identified during MCP integration research:
1. Missing middle layer — 11 broad requirements cover 84 functions, need ~30-40 capability-level sub-requirements
2. Cross-cutting concerns — no mechanism to find things by concern across the tree (proposed: tags on requirements)
3. Applies-to gap — rules trace backward (where they came from) but not forward (what they govern). Broker has indirect system linkage through contracts but no direct applies_to on rules. Proposed: applies_to edge type.
4. Disconnected sources — brainstorming and research docs registered but not wired to requirements.

These problems compound: few requirements means nothing to tag, disconnected sources mean evidence is invisible, no cross-cutting mechanism means more requirements don't help if they can't be found across parents.

DRIFT AUDIT (2026-03-30): Full implementation-vs-provenance audit completed across 6 parallel analysis tracks. Summary: 26/39 rules fully implemented (67%), 5 partial (13%), 8 missing (20%). Core strengths: data model, graph queries, broker sync, scanner/coverage pipeline, and UI canvas are solid implementations matching specs. Critical gaps: (1) Agent identity model (PROV-AUTH-004-009) is entirely unimplemented — 6 rules with zero code, only planning documents. (2) 17/44 Convex mutations lack requirePermission() enforcement (boundaries.ts, topics.ts, questions.ts). (3) CLI ergonomics requirements (filtering, sorting, bulk import/export, domain-identifier lookup) are unbuilt. (4) Phantom source reference RBAC Integration Test points to nothing. (5) provenance prime advertises --reason flag that does not exist. Detailed findings posted to child requirement threads.

DRIFT AUDIT (2026-03-30) — WorkOS SSO Auth: PARTIAL. What works: Every public mutation calls getCurrentUserOrThrow(). createdBy exists on 6/9 artifact tables. WorkOS RBAC pipeline flows to JWT properties.permissions. 27/44 mutations have requirePermission(). Gaps: (1) boundaries, topics, questions tables lack createdBy. (2) messages.sendInternal bypasses auth with optional authorId. (3) 17 mutations in boundaries.ts, topics.ts, questions.ts skip requirePermission — 39% of writes have no fine-grained permission check. (4) Empty permissions array = full write access (auth.ts:290-295). (5) Roles NOT read from JWT, only permissions. Fix: Add createdBy to 3 schemas, add requirePermission to 17 mutations, fix legacy fallthrough.

DRIFT AUDIT (2026-03-30) — RBAC + Agent Identity: 6 rules COMPLETELY MISSING. Zero implementation for PROV-AUTH-004 through 009. No sm_agent_ prefix (only sm_live_ and sm_test_ exist). No agent_tokens table. No agents table in Convex schema. identity_type on AuthContext hardcoded to None everywhere. No delegation tokens. No synthetic Convex user records. No agent-specific role definitions. What exists: Two planning docs (research + implementation plan). The 8 resolutions linked to this requirement describe decisions researched but never implemented. This is the single largest gap between provenance graph state and actual implementation.

DRIFT AUDIT (2026-03-30) — CLI Integration: CRUD 80% complete, usability layer absent. Works: Full create/get/list/update for all 4 artifact types. Graph, traceability, impact commands. Thread post with retry. GetByCode for rules. Missing: (1) No delete on ANY artifact except edges. (2) No --filter or --sort on ANY provenance list command. (3) No bulk import/export. (4) Domain-identifier lookup only for rules, not sources/requirements/resolutions. The 3 standalone CLI ergonomics requirements were filed from real pain but have zero implementation.

DRIFT AUDIT (2026-03-30) — External Test Coverage: SUBSTANTIALLY COMPLETE. Works: Tree-sitter scanner with 6 languages. All 7 PROV-ANNOT rules implemented. Stale annotation validation. coverage scan/status/report. Bulk evidence import. First-class evidence records. Thread diffs on changes only. Minor gaps: (1) PROV-SCAN-011 JSON output diverges from spec (missing project_id, scanned_at; different field names). (2) PROV-COV-020 has 3 subcommands not 4 (--dry-run is a flag). Best-implemented subsystem in the project.

BROKER-AUTH-001: Bearer-only token extraction
Statement: The broker accepts ONLY Bearer tokens in the Authorization header; non-Bearer schemes rejected.
Severity: high | Modality: prohibition
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:141
Description: Checks for Authorization: Bearer <token> format; all other schemes rejected with AuthError::InvalidTokenFormat.

BROKER-AUTH-002: Token type discrimination
Statement: Tokens routed by format: sm_* prefix -> API key; 3-segment dot-separated -> JWT; all others rejected.
Severity: critical | Modality: necessity
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:222, broker/src/auth/client.rs
Description: Prevents type-confusion between API keys and JWTs.

BROKER-AUTH-003: RS256-only JWT validation
Statement: JWTs validated with RS256 only, with issuer, audience, expiration, and kid checks.
Severity: critical | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/jwks.rs:300
Description: Algorithm restricted to RS256 preventing algorithm confusion attacks.

BROKER-AUTH-004: Control plane delegated validation
Statement: API key validity determined exclusively by control plane via POST /auth/validate. Unreachable -> 503, never permissive fallback.
Severity: critical | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/client.rs:183, broker/src/auth/extractor.rs

BROKER-AUTH-005: Hashed cache keys (project-scoped)
Statement: Auth cache keys use hashed tokens with project_id scoping. Raw tokens never stored.
Severity: high | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:250,573

BROKER-AUTH-006: JWKS refresh rate limiting
Statement: JWKS refresh throttled to at most once per 60 seconds.
Severity: medium | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/jwks.rs:363
Description: MIN_REFRESH_INTERVAL_SECS=60. Key rotation via single-retry on KeyNotFound.

BROKER-AUTHZ-001: Scope-based read/write permission gates
Statement: Every handler uses RequirePermission<BrokerRead> or RequirePermission<BrokerWrite> (or fine-grained equivalents). Read endpoints require read scope; mutating endpoints require write scope.
Severity: high | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: every handler in broker/src/api/handlers/ via RequirePermission extractor

BROKER-AUTHZ-002: Hierarchical permission model
Statement: Permission hierarchy: admin > write > read. can() checks exact match, then admin wildcard, then coarse scopes. Empty scopes deny all.
Severity: critical | Modality: necessity
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/models.rs:90

BROKER-AUTHZ-003: Mandatory Auth extractor
Statement: Protected routes use Auth Axum extractor. Handler never invoked if auth fails. Unauthenticated endpoints deliberately omit Auth.
Severity: critical | Modality: necessity
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:587, broker/src/api/server.rs

BROKER-AUTHZ-004: Proposal state machine enforcement
Statement: Intent proposals can only be reviewed when in Pending status. Already-reviewed proposals return 409 Conflict.
Severity: high | Modality: prohibition
Enforcement: broker/src/api/handlers/intent_proposals.rs:469,560,656

BROKER-AUTHZ-005: RequirePermission extractor Statement: RequirePermission

generic Axum extractor enforces fine-grained permissions (32 defined slugs) before handler runs. Denied -> 403. Severity: high | Modality: necessity Enforcement: broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:802, broker/src/auth/permissions.rs

BROKER-AUTHZ-006: JWT fine-grained permission merging
Statement: JWT properties.permissions merged into AuthContext scopes alongside coarse read/write/admin scopes, enabling fine-grained checks for JWT users.
Severity: high | Modality: necessity
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:297

BROKER-TENANT-001: Project-scoped SQL queries
Statement: Every top-level entity query includes WHERE project_id = $1. Data from one project cannot be accessed by another.
Severity: critical | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/storage/mod.rs:3, all repository files

BROKER-TENANT-002: Project allowlist
Statement: X-Statesman-Project header selects project. Must be in allowed_project_ids. Cross-org prevented by org_id check.
Severity: critical | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/extractor.rs, broker/src/auth/models.rs:110

BROKER-TENANT-003: Explicit ownership verification
Statement: Resources fetched by ID verified: resource.project_id == auth.project_id. Defense-in-depth at handler, service, and SQL layers.
Severity: critical | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: 40+ locations across broker/src/services/ and broker/src/storage/

BROKER-TENANT-004: Cross-org prevention
Statement: Project lookups verify project.org_id == auth.org_id. Cached and remote projects both checked.
Severity: critical | Modality: prohibition
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:402, broker/src/services/project_sync.rs:209

BROKER-TENANT-005: Body/auth project_id consistency
Statement: Request body project_id compared against auth.project_id. Mismatches logged as warnings; auth.project_id is authoritative.
Severity: medium | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/api/handlers/rules.rs:843

BROKER-INPUT-001: Progressive transaction validation
Statement: Transactions validated via validate_transaction_progressive() before storage. Errors returned one at a time with progressive disclosure.
Severity: medium | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/api/handlers/transactions.rs:211

BROKER-INPUT-002: Pagination limit capping
Statement: Global clamp_limit(1..=1000) applied to all pagination limits. Evidence endpoint further restricts to 200.
Severity: medium | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/api/types.rs (clamp_limit), broker/src/api/handlers/rules.rs:647

BROKER-INPUT-003: Startup config validation
Statement: AppConfig validated with structural (validator) and business rule checks before server starts. Invalid configs prevent startup.
Severity: medium | Modality: necessity
Enforcement: broker/src/config/app.rs (validate, validate_business_rules)

BROKER-LEAK-001: 404-not-403 for cross-project access
Statement: Cross-project resource access returns 404, not 403. Prevents resource enumeration by probing IDs.
Severity: high | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/api/handlers/contracts.rs, broker/src/services/contract_service.rs

BROKER-LEAK-002: Token prefix-only logging
Statement: Full tokens never logged. token_prefix() truncates to 12 chars. All #[instrument] skip full token values.
Severity: high | Modality: prohibition
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/client.rs:235, broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:224

BROKER-LEAK-003: Generic auth error titles
Statement: Auth errors use RFC 7807 Problem+JSON with generic titles. Detail field may contain diagnostics but title never leaks internals.
Severity: high | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/models.rs:265, broker/src/auth/extractor.rs:663,827, broker/src/api/problem.rs

BROKER-INFRA-001: Request timeout enforcement
Statement: Global 30s timeout via tower TimeoutLayer. DB pool has bounded acquire, idle, and max lifetime timeouts.
Severity: medium | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/api/server.rs:322, broker/src/main.rs:58

BROKER-INFRA-002: Fail-closed on control plane unavailability
Statement: Unreachable control plane -> 503 Service Unavailable. Never falls back to permissive default.
Severity: critical | Modality: prohibition
Enforcement: broker/src/auth/client.rs:86

BROKER-INFRA-003: PostgreSQL-only database
Statement: DATABASE_URL must start with postgres:// or postgresql://. Non-PG URLs cause startup failure.
Severity: medium | Modality: necessity
Enforcement: broker/src/main.rs:47

BROKER-INFRA-004: Parameterized SQL only
Statement: All SQL queries use sqlx parameterized bindings. No string interpolation of user-supplied values.
Severity: critical | Modality: prohibition
Enforcement: broker/src/storage/mod.rs:8, all files in broker/src/storage/

BROKER-AUDIT-001: Structured tracing with project_id
Statement: Every authenticated handler uses #[instrument] with fields(project_id). Sensitive data excluded via skip() directives.
Severity: medium | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: every handler in broker/src/api/handlers/

BROKER-AUDIT-002: Identity attribution on mutations
Statement: Mutating operations record caller identity via auth.user_id_or_agent(). Returns agent/service/user/api_key/anonymous.
Severity: high | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: broker/src/api/handlers/intent_proposals.rs, intent.rs, rules.rs, contracts.rs

BROKER-AUDIT-003: Content-addressed deduplication
Statement: Contract versions use SHA-256 content hashing of type definitions. Identical types produce same version ID.
Severity: medium | Modality: obligation
Enforcement: core/src/deduplication/hasher.rs (compute_contract_content_hash), broker/src/services/transaction_service.rs (caller)

Awaiting human review and approval of resolutions before creating rules (Phase 2).
Source: k172qj7g2end8s8a1yqte0yqk984ee88 (Broker Source Code)

Rich review: Directionally sound. Keep the model append-only and fact-oriented: persona profiles are versioned values, runs are execution facts, comments are attributable observations, proposals are candidate artifacts, and decisions are separate promoted facts. Do not let synthesis rewrite discussion into truth; promotion should create new canonical artifacts with traceability back to comments, evidence, dissent, and run metadata. Idempotency should preserve immutable attempt records and visible partial failure, not hide retries behind mutation.

Jony review: The model is clear and productively restrained. Preserve that restraint: named personas should clarify perspective, not become theatre. Keep the reading hierarchy simple: artifact context, bounded discussion, attributable comments, synthesis, unresolved dissent. The human promotion gate is essential; ideation can be plural, but canonical artifacts must remain curated. Consider adding an explicit readability constraint: synthesis should surface evidence, risks, and minority positions without collapsing them into false consensus.

Linus review: This is mostly sane, but do not let it become an unbounded chat-log generator. Make the invariants boring and enforceable: stable ideation job keys, versioned persona identities, deterministic participant slots, retry-safe write tokens, explicit partial/failure state, and hard limits on participants, turns, bytes/tokens, deadlines, and retention. Raw persona output should remain evidence, not canonical truth; promotion must stay human-gated. Preserve dissent and source grounding in structured synthesis, not transcript sludge. The link to the existing thread reliability requirement is correct; treat it as an implementation dependency, not just traceability decoration.

Steve review: Strong foundation, but do not let this become agents role-playing in a thread. The magic is: show me what I am missing. Keep personas as quiet product lenses, not theater. Human promotion and dissent preservation are exactly right. Idempotency and partial failure are table stakes, not user-facing concepts. Naming like PROV-IDEATE is fine internally, but the product should feel simpler: Review, Challenge, Synthesize. If this produces sharper requirements with less noise, it is useful. If it produces clever debate transcripts, it is a toy.

Charity review: Directionally good, but productionability hinges on one thing: can we explain a run from a single ID? Require a stable ideation_run_id or correlation ID on every job, persona execution, thread comment, synthesis, retry, partial failure, and promoted artifact. To debug why a bad recommendation happened, preserve persona version, prompt/input artifact versions, model/tool metadata, evidence retrieved, dissent considered, synthesis rationale, retry attempts, idempotency key, and post status. Partial writes and failed persona posts must be visible in-thread, not buried in logs.

Jony review: The model is clear and productively restrained. Preserve that restraint: named personas should clarify perspective, not become theatre. Keep the reading hierarchy simple: artifact context, bounded discussion, attributable comments, synthesis, unresolved dissent. The human promotion gate is essential; ideation can be plural, but canonical artifacts must remain curated. Consider adding an explicit readability constraint: synthesis should surface evidence, risks, and minority positions without collapsing them into false consensus.

New evidence from multi-persona ideation requirement review on 2026-06-14: five review comments were posted concurrently to parent requirement jn740dkdkezgqy75an6k1nky7h88mh5r. The convenience thread post path created two active threads for the same parent at nearly the same creation time: canonical thread k57463khgmxxjd4hkf5ns0bypd88mnb6 and duplicate thread k5770r1jagqweqq7h31xt8jxx988nrz5. The split required manually reposting one comment into the canonical thread and resolving the duplicate. This confirms a concrete race in find-or-create thread posting and strengthens the need for idempotent thread creation / unique active thread semantics before automated multi-agent jobs rely on this path.

Backfill note: Expanded PROV-IDEATE from six broad rules to a noscope-style validation-grade rule set. Added subrequirements for first-class run lifecycle, structured participant contributions, proposal cards, outcome-first thread presentation, governance limits, and validation hooks. Added rules PROV-IDEATE-007 through PROV-IDEATE-038 covering correlation IDs, status lifecycle, input snapshots, deterministic participant slots, evidence labels, synthesis schema, proposal lifecycle, scope/redaction/budget controls, one-active-thread concurrency, and acceptance tests for concurrency, retries, partial failure, promotion traceability, run inspection, and output schema validation.

Implemented persona profile registry and attribution metadata for ideation participants in statesman-p44g.3. Added versioned persona profile registry validation, persona labels and prompt template versions, owning attribution, server-derived executor identity/model/timestamp/target output metadata, message binding/authorship validation, and separation checks for persona, owner, and executor identities. Verified with focused Convex tests, TypeScript, Convex validation, Linus review consensus, and Steve product review consensus.
